summary: Gruber reviews Apple's September 2020 'Time Flies' event, covering the Apple Watch Series 6, Apple Watch SE, new iPad, iPad Air, and the Apple One services bundle. He characterizes the Series 6 as a textbook example of Apple's incremental update strategy, noting how yearly improvements compound for upgraders. The iPad Air stands out for debuting the A14 chip and introducing Touch ID in the power button — a feature he notes many iPhone users would prefer over Face ID during the pandemic. He validates his own earlier Apple One pricing prediction, calling the bundle a smart play to expand Apple's subscriber base rather than maximize revenue from existing customers. He also praises the virtual event format's production quality while arguing it can't fully replace live events.
key_points: Apple Watch Series 6 exemplifies Apple's incremental strategy: no single breakthrough, but the cumulative upgrades from a Series 3 to Series 6 are substantial

The iPad Air is technically the most interesting announcement, debuting the A14 chip before the iPhone 12 and introducing Touch ID in the power button — a feature pandemic mask-wearers would love on iPhones
Apple One's pricing landed almost exactly where Gruber predicted: $15/individual, $20/family, targeting the 80% of iPhone owners who pay for zero Apple subscriptions
The Series 3 remaining in the lineup is a burden on developers forced to support its older display dimensions for years to come
Virtual keynotes have impeccable production values but lack the nuance of live events, though they may reshape how Apple handles smaller product introductions
money_quotes: The thing about rolling out incremental updates every single year is they add up — and people don't buy new watches every year.
It was clearly the model Darth Vader would wear.
It seems all but certain that next year — if civilization still exists — the Apple Watch SE will move down to the $199 spot in the lineup.
In the same way the new iPhone SE is (I think) the last of the home-button iPhones, this new iPad is (I think) the last of the home-button iPads.
It's interesting to think that the mid-range iPad now has a feature millions of people would rather see in high-end iPhones.
Apple's goal with a services bundle isn't to get the most money it can from you, individually, but to get the most money it can from all of us, collectively.
If you're in the Apple ecosystem and want Apple Music, it's a no-brainer to pay $5 more to get the Apple One bundle instead.
It's a simple proposition at a compelling price — exactly what a bundled offering should be.
key_insight: Apple's September 2020 event was defined not by any single breakthrough but by strategic product positioning — incremental hardware improvements that compound over upgrade cycles and a services bundle priced to convert the 80% of iPhone owners who currently pay Apple nothing.
